title: A Computer Just Found 10,000 Hidden Planets — and One Is a 'Hot Jupiter'!
summary: Imagine you're looking at a sky full of stars, and you notice one wink — just a tiny flicker. That wink could be a planet passing in front of it! Scientists have been searching for these winks for years, but some stars are so dim that the winks are almost invisible. So a team of astronomers taught a computer to spot them. They fed the computer light measurements from 83 million stars captured by a space telescope called TESS. And guess what? The computer found 11,554 possible planets — and over 10,000 of them had never been seen before! If all these are real planets, we'd go from knowing about 6,000 exoplanets (planets outside our solar system) to nearly 18,000. That's like suddenly finding out your neighborhood has three times as many houses as you thought! To prove their computer was right, the scientists used a huge telescope in Chile to check one candidate. They found a real planet — a 'hot Jupiter,' a giant gas ball that's super close to its star. It's 3,950 light-years away, which means the light we see left that star before the pyramids were built! Most of these new planets zip around their stars in less than a month, so they're probably too hot for life. But the discovery shows that with clever computers, we can uncover hidden worlds right under our noses. Who knows what else is winking at us from deep space?
why_it_matters: Every new world found in space shows how much is left to discover – maybe one day you will use smart tools like this to find a planet where life could exist.

keywords.explanation: Astronomers are scientists who study stars, planets, and everything in space.
Exoplanets are planets that orbit stars outside our own solar system.
A hot Jupiter is a huge gas planet, like Jupiter, that orbits very close to its star and gets extremely hot.
A light‑year is the distance light travels in one year – about 6 trillion miles – used to measure space.
A space telescope is a telescope that floats above Earth’s atmosphere to take clearer pictures of space.
In astronomy, a candidate is a possible planet that still needs to be checked to make sure it is real.

questions.question: What space telescope provided the light measurements from 83 million stars?
How many possible planets did the computer find in total?
How many of those planet candidates were brand‑new discoveries?
What kind of actual planet did the scientists confirm with a telescope in Chile?
How far away is the confirmed hot Jupiter from Earth?
About how many exoplanets were known before this discovery?

background_read: TESS (the Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite) is a NASA space telescope launched in 2018. It stares at huge patches of the sky to catch tiny dips in starlight when a planet passes in front of its star.
Exoplanets are worlds outside our solar system, and before the 1990s nobody even knew if they existed; today over 5,000 are confirmed.
A hot Jupiter is a giant gassy planet, like Jupiter, but it orbits so close to its star that temperatures can reach thousands of degrees, making it inhospitable for life as we know it.

A light‑year measures distance, not time: one light‑year is about 9 trillion kilometers (6 trillion miles). The light from the hot Jupiter left its star around the time the ancient pyramids were built on Earth.
Article_Structure: WHO: A team of astronomers and a clever computer program.
WHAT: The computer scanned light from 83 million stars and found over 11,500 possible planets, including a real hot Jupiter confirmed by a telescope in Chile.
WHERE: The star data came from the TESS space telescope; follow‑up checks used a large telescope in Chile.
WHY: Astronomers wanted to uncover hidden planets from stars too dim for human eyes to spot, using the computer to find faint winks that reveal planetary crossings.

perspectives.description: They are thrilled because computers can speed up discoveries, potentially doubling or tripling the number of known exoplanets very quickly.
They remind everyone that not all candidates will turn out to be real planets; some winks could be caused by starspots or instrument errors, so each one must be double‑checked.

Seeing how computers can sift through millions of data points, experts believe similar methods could be used to find not just planets but also asteroids, comets, and other space objects hiding in the dark.
